text: Welcome to this video of a laparoscopic hydroplasty combined with a fundoplication. We'll show you the basics of our technique. After the reduction of the hernia, we'll perform a dorsal choreography, then we'll place the mesh and we'll add a fundoplication.
The surgeon identifies the hiatal hernia and begins dissection at the dorsal aspects, moving towards the right crus of the diaphragm.

The surgeon dissects the ventral aspects of the hernia defect towards the angle of His, carefully removing dense adhesions from the left crus. The distal esophagus is dissected, revealing the size of the hernia. The procedure shifts back to the right side to remove additional adhesions, with caution taken to avoid damaging the visible vagal nerve. The left crus is exposed during the dissection.
The surgeon removes the remaining adhesions at the angle of His on the left side, allowing for full mobilization of the distal oesophagus and complete reduction of the hiatal hernia.

The surgeon prepares for dorsal choreography by placing a 32-gauge gastric tube. Two to three non-absorbable sutures are placed between the crura of the diaphragm, ensuring sufficient tissue is included to prevent muscle tearing. The second stitch is positioned approximately one centimeter dorsally from the first.

The surgeon closes the gap and prepares to introduce a synthetic bioabsorbable mesh through a twelve millimeter trocar. The mesh is wrapped around the distal esophagus, featuring an opening for the esophagus and remaining open ventrally.

The surgeon tucks the synthetic bioabsorbable mesh underneath the surrounding tissue, positioning it on the diaphragm's crura. The mesh is then fixed by attaching its right side to the ventral part of the right crus and similarly on the left side, using one stitch per side for ventral fixation.
The surgeon places a final stitch dorsally to the right cruse, completing the fixation of the mesh around the distal esophagus.
The surgeon places a stay suture on the mobilized fundus and uses a gold finger instrument from the right side to pull the fundus over behind the distal esophagus, preparing for the fundoplication.

The surgeon successfully retrieves the opened stay suture and pulls it over to the right side. The fundus is attached to the ventral lateral aspect of the distal esophagus for a 270-degree fundoplication. The first of three non-resorbable stitches is placed on the right side, followed by a second stitch positioned approximately one centimeter from the first.
The surgeon places three single stitches on the left side, each approximately one centimeter apart, mirroring the technique used on the right side.
The surgeon ties the last knot ventrally and places an additional stitch posteriorly to the dorsal aspect of the right crus, completing the fixation process.
